title: P0 SPIKE: drive §6 dark-factory-pipeline via native .mcp.json / stdio MCP registration (bypassing claude-agent-acp _meta + -alwaysload alias path). Mechanical swap: McpServer.layerStdio over FiregridAgentToolkit + .mcp.json in run cwd. Falsifiable: tools/call > 0 ⇒ green §6 + cause #1 localized to ACP _meta plumbing; tools/call = 0 ⇒ MCP-path eliminated as variable, §9g instrumentation scope narrows to #2/#4/#5. Baseline = tf-3ek (PR #441). Hours, not days.
description:
# P0 SPIKE — native MCP registration path for §6 dark-factory

Baseline: tf-3ek / PR #441 (codec→SDK source-verified findings; recent amendment owed: ruling for cause #1 was scoped to the ACP-mediated path only).

## Hypothesis (falsifiable)

The §6 `tools/call=0` symptom may be caused (in whole or in part) by claude-agent-acp's `_meta.claudeCode.options.mcpServers` plumbing — specifically the `-alwaysload` alias hack and the spread/merge with `acpDerived` (acp-agent.js:1438). The model may not be recognizing prefix-renamed `mcp__firegrid-alwaysload__<tool>` names as primary, and/or the merge ordering may be producing a tool list the `claude_code` preset system prompt does not steer toward.

If we register Firegrid MCP tools the **documented user-facing way** (`.mcp.json` and/or `claude mcp add`) instead of through the ACP `_meta` channel, the tools are advertised under their natural names (`mcp__firegrid__<tool>`), loaded by `claude-agent-sdk` natively via `settingSources: ["project", "local"]` (already configured at acp-agent.js:1414), and the SDK's `enableAllProjectMcpServers` / `enabledMcpjsonServers` settings (sdk.d.ts:4023-4029) approve them as a first-class citizen.

## Acceptance criteria

### Implementation

1. Add a stdio MCP shim entrypoint that swaps `McpServer.layerHttp` for `McpServer.layerStdio` over the existing `FiregridAgentToolkit`. Pattern source: `packages/host-sdk/src/host/mcp-host.ts`; layer source: `repos/effect/packages/ai/ai/src/McpServer.ts:533`.
2. Wire `--context-id <ctx>` argv → `requireLocalContext` so contextId routing matches the existing HTTP path's `/mcp/runtime-context/:contextId` authority semantics.
3. In tiny-firegrid's dark-factory-pipeline simulation driver, write a per-session `.mcp.json` into the agent's cwd registering Firegrid via the stdio shim, with `alwaysLoad: true` set as a first-class field (NOT via the `-alwaysload` alias hack). Optionally also `disableBuiltInTools: true` via `.claude/settings.json` for fidelity.
4. Drive `simulate:run -- dark-factory-pipeline` against this path. Codec ACP `_meta` `mcpServers` payload should be EMPTY (or the path should run without it at all) so this is a clean MCP-path comparison, not double-advertisement.

### Decision rule

**GREEN (`tools/call > 0`, plan-step turn-completions observed):**
- §6 is end-to-end working via the documented user-facing MCP path.
- Cause #1 is **localized** to claude-agent-acp `_meta` plumbing — naming, merge ordering, or both. The fix path is "stop using the `_meta` channel; use `.mcp.json` directly." That's a Firegrid-codec change, fully in our control.
- File a follow-up bead for either deleting the codec's `_meta` hack entirely OR keeping it as a fallback for non-claude-code agents that DON'T support `.mcp.json` (codex-acp, etc.).

**RED (`tools/call = 0` still):**
- MCP registration path is **eliminated as a variable**. The §6 gap lives elsewhere — cause #2 (tool_choice absent from SDK), #4 (system prompt steering, but locked structurally), or #5 (streaming tool_use harness race).
- The §9g instrumentation lane's search space narrows to those three; the wire capture work doesn't need to vary MCP advertisement to localize.

Either outcome is **forward progress**. Time budget: hours, not days.

## Non-goals

- Do NOT also try to "fix" tool_choice in this spike. The SDK doesn't expose it; this spike answers a different question. If the spike goes green, tool_choice becomes moot for §6. If red, tool_choice work is the §9g lane's, not ours.
- Do NOT remove the existing codec `_meta` path in this spike. Wire the spike alongside it so we can revert cleanly. Removal/rationalization happens in a follow-up bead after the spike's verdict.
- Do NOT vendor claude-agent-acp / claude-agent-sdk. The 60-second-grep against npx-cached source is sufficient for this spike; vendoring is the §9g lane's responsibility.

## Affected files (approx scope)

- New: `packages/host-sdk/src/host/mcp-host-stdio.ts` (or similar) — McpServer.layerStdio composition
- New: `packages/host-sdk/src/bin/firegrid-mcp-stdio.ts` (or similar) — argv entry, --context-id parsing
- Edit: `packages/tiny-firegrid/src/simulations/dark-factory-pipeline/driver.ts` — write per-session .mcp.json into cwd, drive agent with that cwd, optionally clear the codec `_meta` mcpServers payload for this run
- Possibly edit: `packages/runtime/src/agent-event-pipeline/codecs/acp/index.ts` — only if the codec needs a "do not inject _meta.mcpServers" mode flag for the spike; preferred is to keep the codec unchanged and just empty the input

## Dependencies / coordination

- Coordinator-aware. Does NOT conflict with tf-slb (Shape C wait_router) or tf-la7 (ProjectionStream brand) — those are wait-taxonomy substrate work, this is codec/MCP boundary.
- Does NOT conflict with the §9g instrumentation lane (when dispatched). They answer different questions; the spike's outcome narrows or unblocks §9g's scope.

## Cross-references

- tf-3ek (PR #441) — codec→SDK source-verified baseline
- tf-549 — terminal finding on forced tool-choice (re-confirms cause #2 verdict regardless of spike outcome)
- COORDINATOR_HANDOFF_s6_dark_factory.md §1, §9g — instrumentation framing (the spike COMPLEMENTS this; it does not supersede)
- packages/host-sdk/src/host/mcp-host.ts — existing HTTP composition pattern to mirror for stdio
- repos/effect/packages/ai/ai/src/McpServer.ts:533 — layerStdio implementation
- @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk@0.3.143/sdk.d.ts:4023-4029 — .mcp.json approval settings (verified)
- @agentclientprotocol/claude-agent-acp@0.36.1 acp-agent.js:1414 — settingSources already requests project/local

title: External-MCP header credential gap: forbid literal secrets in mcpServers headers (+ ref form)
description: Per tf-x1jx (PR #562, merged 02b73365), the real near-term correctness gap: RuntimeConfig.mcpServers {headers} carry LITERAL secrets into the durable plane, unlike ref-only RuntimeEnvBinding. The agent's external-MCP attachment (Smithery namespace token, provider API keys) lands as plaintext in durable rows. FIX (do regardless of the A/B disposition): forbid literal header secrets — validation rejects plaintext secrets in mcpServers headers (minimal beta fix) — AND/OR add a ref form mirroring RuntimeEnvBinding (the durable path). Beta-minimal = the forbid/validation; ref-form is the fuller fix. Mirror the existing RuntimeEnvBinding ref pattern. Substrate-neutral: Firegrid holds refs, not secrets.

title: Live-validate schedule_me with true future timestamp and exact-once prompt delivery
description:
Investigation reference: docs/investigations/2026-05-21-acp-parent-child-output-channel-gap.md
Related merged fix context: schedule_me live timeout work / PR #637.

The latest Zed run asked the agent to schedule a prompt about 15 seconds in the future, but the agent had no clock-read tool and supplied when=1779386415000, which was already in the past relative to the captured trace time around 1779419924xxx ms. This validates that overdue schedule_me calls return quickly and fire promptly, but it does not validate true future durable scheduling.

Required work: run or automate a live validation that computes the target timestamp from the host clock, e.g. Date.now()+15000, rather than relying on the agent's estimated wall clock. Confirm schedule_me returns {scheduled:true} quickly, the current turn completes, and the scheduled prompt arrives exactly once after the deadline. If practical, include replay/resume or restart coverage to check that future delivery is not duplicated or lost.

Acceptance criteria:
- A documented trace or test artifact shows a true future timestamp greater than the host current time at scheduling.
- The scheduled prompt is not delivered before the due time and is delivered once after the due time.
- The result is recorded in a short finding/test note with enough trace details to distinguish this from an overdue/immediate schedule.

STOP/EXIT instruction: if true-future validation reveals duplicate prompts, no wakeup after host restart/resume, or a need to redesign scheduler/workflow-engine ownership beyond a harness/test fix, exit the task and report the exact architecture boundary and observed failure mode rather than marking validation complete.
close_reason: Merged PR #642

title: GAP(dark-factory): no public agent-facing read of caller-owned DurableTable rows/projections
description:
cca2/tf-se0.1 hit this building the dark-factory loop. Needed: the planner agent to read the app-owned factory.trigger.accepted DurableTable row as durable context. The public session facade exposes createOrLoad/prompt/start/snapshot/wait (runtime observations) + permission responses, but NOT an advertised tool/wait/source binding for arbitrary caller-owned DurableTable rows/projections. cca2 is proceeding via a config-local app-led escape hatch (driver reads the durable row, prompts planner with the payload) — NO apps/factory workaround, NO private import.

TRIAGE (coordinator, FINDINGS_TRIAGE_RUBRIC): cat-1/2 substrate-EXPRESSIBILITY gap — real production model: the factory-vision choreography needs the planner to read caller-owned durable facts through the PUBLIC surface; it cannot today. NOT a toy artifact, NOT tooling. P2 default (Gurdas re-prioritizes). Associated-with tf-se0 (discovered-by); NON-blocking — cca2 continues on the escape hatch, this is a captured finding for prioritization, not a build gate.
close_reason: RETRACTED — toy over-narrow framing, NOT a public-surface gap. Planner/implementer sessions launch with runtimeContextMcp enabled and host-sdk mcp-host provides the default Firegrid choreography toolset to the running agent (demonstrated by codex-acp-tool-call-pipeline: runtimeContextMcp { enabled: true } + host bound MCP listener). The agent reads/waits via runtime-context MCP tools; the capability is NOT missing through the public surface. Coordinator re-triage: cat-3 (test-fixture-shaped, no real consumer wall) — supersedes the original cat-2. A narrower PRECISE finding may be filed later ONLY if the public tool/channel schema cannot express a specific factory row wait/read cca2 actually needs.

title: INV-6: tiny-firegrid sim — Activity α/β/γ shape comparison (validates future Activity-rethink SDD direction)
description:
Build THREE parallel sub-sims, each prototyping a different Activity shape:
  α — Activity.streamed(name, schema)((seed) => Stream<A>) — engine tracks emit cursor
  β — Activity.subscribed(name, sourceSchema)((event) => Effect<void>) — engine owns subscription + last-ack cursor
  γ — Activity.folded(name, stateSchema, sourceSchema)(seed, step) — engine maintains durable folded state

Each sub-sim implements the SAME use case (agent waits for first matching fact on a CallerFact stream + emits a marker on match) but using a different Activity primitive shape. Compare:
(a) Lines of code in the workflow body
(b) Restart-replay span shape (engine work on resume)
(c) Observability span density
(d) Engine implementation surface needed (if any extension beyond today's Activity)

Acceptance: FINDING ranks α/β/γ by ergonomic cleanliness + restart-replay cost; recommends one to pursue OR confirms today's Activity-as-value-terminator + Stream.runHead is the right call.

Estimated work: ~1-1.5 days. LOWER priority than inv-1 through inv-5; this is for the future Activity-rethink SDD, not the current one-substrate collapse.

title: TFIND-041: ToolUse event lifecycle is under-discriminated (decision B)
description:
Area: runtime / agent-event contract.

Status: resolved (#336, e361d4147 — decision B + by-decision doc-comment).

Triage: cat-1 (rubric anchor — real architectural shape question; resolution = right-sized by-decision doc-comment, zero behavior change).

ToolUse is normalized as a shared AgentOutputEvent, but execution authority is not carried by the event. ACP and stdio-jsonl both emit ToolUse, while workflow core interprets it by consulting codec/session mode.

DECIDED (Gurdas 2026-05-18): (B) Session-mode authority, made explicit. Session/codec mode owns the ToolUse execution lifecycle by decision, not by default. The canonical decision is now recorded in-code (#336, doc-comment at the agentProtocol === 'acp' branch): ACP = observation-only, stdio-jsonl = client-result roundtrip, the AgentOutput ToolUse event is deliberately NOT discriminated by execution authority, and the (A) event-level discriminant is a tracked, deliberately-deferred future option.
close_reason: Resolved by #336 — decision B + by-decision doc-comment landed.

title: P0: source-verify and fix schedule_me live ACP timeout
description: Follow-up from docs/investigations/2026-05-21-acp-stdio-edge-permission-deadlock.md §6. After the PermissionRequest deadlock fix (#628/tf-46i4), schedule_me still times out independently. Evidence: permission ratio is clean, schedule_me handler starts and schedules a clock wakeup, but the MCP tools/call never completes and the ACP edge times out. Source-verify the lowering path in host-sdk agent-tools execution, identify whether self-re-entry/prompt append collides with the in-flight turn, and patch with focused regression evidence. Do not conflate with permission handling.
close_reason: Source-verified finding merged in PR #632; implementation split to tf-5ose.

comments.author: gnijor
comments.text: SOURCE-VERIFIED root cause (DRAFT PR #632, finding doc). schedule_me lowers to runtime-agent-tool-execution.ts:219 schedule = DurableClock.sleep(delayMs).zipRight(append), AWAITED inline by the ToolCallWorkflow (toolkit-layer handleTool blocks on the workflow result). So the tool + the agent turn are held open until the scheduled time. delayMs = when - now; when is ABSOLUTE epoch ms computed by the LLM (schema.ts:543) -> any when beyond the 30s turn timeout times the edge out before the durable sleep fires. Trace: schedule_me tools/call never completes while sleep's does (sole diff = inline sleep+append). NOT permission (clean ratio), NOT primarily append-collision. FIX = non-blocking replay-safe durable schedule (return {scheduled:true} immediately; fire later). forkDaemon/host-scope Clock.sleep shortcuts re-fork on ToolCallWorkflow replay -> duplicate prompts (the tf-7kq8/tf-e49h replay class) -> REJECTED. Correct: separate durable ScheduledPromptWorkflow (idempotencyKey=scheduleId) owning DurableClock.sleep + idempotent appendRuntimeIngress, started fire-and-forget. That is a substrate design change (new workflow + engine reg + host-cap provision in body), needs trace validation (deterministic sim, future when: tool returns immediately, turn completes, prompt fires once). Did NOT ship a rushed shortcut. Live Zed retest only after the durable workflow lands. Recommend implementing as the next focused change.

title: Split codec _meta payload: separate disableBuiltInTools from MCP advertisement; replace _meta.mcpServers with .mcp.json writer at agent cwd (per tf-s8y verdict)
description:
Post-spike (tf-s8y) verdict-driven follow-up. Production codec change.

## Context

tf-s8y proved the native .mcp.json MCP-registration path works end-to-end:
- Agent invoked `mcp__firegrid__wait_for` via natural name (not the `-alwaysload` alias)
- McpServer.initialize + tools/list + 3× /runtime-context/:contextId POSTs confirmed SDK-side discovery
- 3 tool_call session_updates emitted by agent

## What this bead splits

The codec's `_meta.claudeCode` payload currently bundles TWO orthogonal concerns:
1. **MCP server advertisement** — `_meta.claudeCode.options.mcpServers` with the `-alwaysload` alias hack to bypass the acpDerived spread merge ordering
2. **Tool-policy** — `_meta.disableBuiltInTools: true` to strip Read/Bash/Grep/Glob etc

tf-s8y dropped (1) and accidentally dropped (2) — the agent then used built-in Read on a memory file because nothing prevented it.

## Acceptance criteria

- Replace codec's `_meta.claudeCode.options.mcpServers` injection with a `.mcp.json` writer at the agent's cwd boundary (the codec adapter at the start path, before sandbox spawn)
- Keep `_meta.disableBuiltInTools: true` OR migrate it to a project-local `.claude/settings.json` write — but make it SEPARATE from the MCP advertisement
- Re-run `simulate:run -- dark-factory` and confirm:
  - Agent has no built-in tool fallback (no Read/Bash/Grep/Glob calls)
  - Firegrid MCP tools registered under natural `mcp__firegrid__*` names
  - tool_call session_updates targeting Firegrid tools

## Affected files

- packages/runtime/src/agent-event-pipeline/codecs/acp/index.ts (claudeAgentAcpAlwaysLoadMeta + callers)
- packages/host-sdk/src/host/runtime-context-session/codec-adapter.ts (resolveEffectiveMcpServers + new pre-spawn .mcp.json writer)
- packages/host-sdk/src/host/runtime-context-session/mcp-json-writer.ts (new — pure fn cwd+contextId+baseUrl → .mcp.json)
- packages/tiny-firegrid/src/simulations/dark-factory/driver.ts (revert spike-time per-session cwd + manual .mcp.json write once codec owns it)

## Dependencies

- Baseline: tf-3ek / PR #441 (codec→SDK source-verified findings)
- Spike: tf-s8y / PR #444 (verdict GREEN)
close_reason: STALE — closing. PR #446 (the branch this tracked) is CLOSED ('FOUNDATIONAL DOCS DONT LOSE THESE'); assignee 'ola' is a defunct lane label from a prior wave. The codec _meta split / .mcp.json work it described was superseded by the merged tf-x1jx external-MCP design (provider tools via RuntimeConfig.mcpServers; MCP-tools-first). No live work; un-stranding the bead graph.

comments.author: coordinator
comments.text:
WAVE-2A VERDICT IN 2026-05-20 (tf-fugl PR #465 draft): GREEN-zip-2-routing-confirmed; CORRELATION-NEEDS-WAVE-2C-RESOLUTION.

Routing: PermissionRequest IS in runtimeOutputStream (output seq 7, trace lines 453-454); permission response IS in runtimeInputStream (input seq 1, lines 462/469). TWO-STREAM shape suffices for permission handling — NO third stream needed.

Correlation/pairing nuance (not a routing gap; deferred to Wave-2C): INV-1's zip body pairs input seq 1 against Ready/Status rather than request seq 7 (lines 501/529). This is the zipLatest 'pairs latest from each side' behavior — the request appears AFTER an earlier input emission, so zipLatest emits (input seq 1, output seq 7) rather than (matching response, matching request). Whether the stream-zip handler treats that as a NEW pair to handle, or ignores re-emissions of older seq numbers, depends on Wave-2C's empty-side semantics + correlation findings.

Phase 1 Lane 1 SHAPE locked to GREEN-zip-2: permission responses fold into runtimeInputStream; no third stream. Lane 1 still gated on Wave-2C verdict for correlation semantics (seq tracking / sentinel seeding / etc.).

comments.author: coordinator
comments.text:
WAVE-2B VERDICTS IN 2026-05-20 (tf-yib4 PR #466 draft):

RESTART-REPLAY: GREEN — Stream.zipLatest(...).runForEach(...) inside runtime-context workflow body continues correctly across scoped host bounce. Phase-1 Lane 1 CAN claim restart-replay coverage.

REC-3 SCENARIO (c): HANDLER-NEEDS-SEQ-TRACKING — under Activity retry or host bounce, the journal re-emits earlier output rows, and the stream-zip handler WILL see duplicates without explicit sequence tracking. Phase-1 Lane 1 (runtime-context body) MUST add sequence-tracking in the handler to dedup re-emissions. This compounds with Wave-2A's correlation finding (zipLatest pairs latest-from-each-side, including older seq numbers from one side).

Phase 1 Lane 1 shape now locked to:
- Two-stream GREEN-zip-2 (Wave-2A): permission responses fold into runtimeInputStream; no third stream
- Stream-zip restart-replay viable (Wave-2B): no body redesign needed
- Handler MUST track sequence numbers to skip pairs containing already-processed rows (Wave-2B scenario c)

REMAINING GATE: Wave-2C verdict (zipLatest empty-side semantics) determines whether Lane 1 needs to seed sentinels for empty-side handling at startup.

title: Production cutover: workflow-owned RuntimeContext input table
description: Implement the production workflow-owned RuntimeContext input table cutover using the P3 row shape from PR #653 / SDD_RUNTIME_CONTEXT_WORKFLOW_INPUT_TABLE_CUTOVER Amendment 1. Producers write idempotent input rows keyed by inputId via existing insertOrGet; RuntimeContextWorkflow assigns dense processing ordinals on consume and advances a workflow-owned durable input cursor. Scope first slice to table/schema + cursor + one bounded channel write/read path if possible. No write-time dense sequence allocation, no nextInputSequenceToAssign, no inputIds side index, no CAS/atomic append precondition, no external-await/deferred bridge. Still gated on F3 wakeup proof/implementation and legacy mailbox deletion sequencing. STOP if the P3 consume-time cursor requires a second source of truth, inputs.query on replay path, CAS/transaction, or a broad lifecycle rewrite.
notes: STOP (resume 2026-05-22, comment): branch refreshed clean off main+#658. Empirical probe: durable-stream offsets are NON-DENSE byte positions (logical +141/event), so "point-read arrival by offset" is impossible — offset is a total order, not a dense key. Arrival consume must be a FORWARD-ORDERED READ. Two candidate designs (producer offset-index w/ crash hole via Found-has-no-offset; or raw-stream forward read), both materially different from the stated/amended design and unvalidated. Do NOT code the correctness-critical input replay arm blind. Block on a P3-B CONSUME derisk in tiny-firegrid (existing tiny-input-append-wakeup is the SUPERSEDED write-time shape) + Amendment §A2 fix. #658 offset + #651 suspend/resume remain valid building blocks.

comments.author: gnijor
comments.text:
STOP — genuinely multi-stage; no small direct cutover available. Stage A source-of-truth cutover is gated on TWO non-existent durable primitives, and the SDD itself mandates they be filed as separate prerequisite beads BEFORE Stage A starts.

SOURCE-GROUNDED BLOCKERS:
1. NO atomic multi-row append primitive (SDD prereq #2 / deletion-bead #1). packages/effect-durable-operators/src/DurableTable.ts exposes only per-row insert/insertOrGet/upsert/delete/get/query/subscribe + table-level awaitTxId. insertOrGet is documented 'not a lock, claim, mutex, semaphore, lease, or coordination primitive.' No transaction at store/state-protocol level either. The SDD's appendRuntimeContextWorkflowInput needs an atomic op across 3 collections (inputIds read + contexts.nextInputSequenceToAssign read/update + inputs insert + inputIds insert). Cannot be built within tf-vrz6 without first adding a new durable-substrate transaction primitive (its own SDD/bead). SDD: 'File the missing atomic input append primitive as the blocker instead of rebuilding request/claim rows.'

2. NO F3 wakeup (SDD deletion-bead #3). engine-runtime.ts WorkflowEngine has no signal; only deferredDone/interrupt/resume. A table write cannot wake a suspended RuntimeContextWorkflowNative. Current wakeup IS engine.deferredDone on runtimeInputDeferredFor (runtime-input-deferred.ts:140). Removing it needs engine.signal or table-write-driven resume. SDD 'Cannot Land Before F3': 'Make a workflow wait only on table rows if table writes cannot wake it.'

CURRENT BOUNDARY (the seam to cut):
- channel binding -> RuntimeControlPlaneTable.inputIntents (protocol/launch/host-control-request.ts appendInputIntent)
- single host-scoped RuntimeInputIntentDispatcherLive (kernel/runtime-context-workflow-runtime.ts) -> appendRuntimeInputDeferred
- appendRuntimeInputDeferred (runtime-input-deferred.ts:94) scans WorkflowEngineTable.deferreds, computes sequence via reduce(max(row.sequence+1),0) [the SDD-FORBIDDEN max+1 pattern, runtime-input-deferred.ts:126-130], then engine.deferredDone = assignment AND wakeup in one.
- RuntimeContextWorkflowNative awaits runtimeInputDeferredFor (runtime-context.ts:203-229,803,823).
Atomic-sequence safety today comes from the SINGLE dispatcher serialization (BOUNDARIES.7), NOT a table primitive.

WHY NO before-F3 slice ships here: the only before-F3 landable work (schema + Layer wiring + non-authoritative mirror writes + tests + static checks) is NOT a cutover. It RAISES N (new module nodes) with NO credited SCC break, and is a transactional-cutover-rule half-ship (canon). It would not advance tf-9rpy's SCC-gone goal — the SCC break only happens when the body->engine deferred-await edge is removed, which requires F3 + the delete-append-deferred bead.

PROPOSED SLICE PLAN (re-sequence tf-vrz6):
- NEW bead A 'runtime-input-atomic-append-and-addressing' (SDD deletion-bead #1): add the atomic appendRuntimeContextWorkflowInput primitive + (contextId,sequence) point-address. NOTE: needs a new DurableTable transaction/conditional-update primitive => likely its own substrate SDD. BLOCKS source-of-truth writes.
- NEW bead B 'runtime-input-engine-signal-wakeup' (SDD deletion-bead #3 / F3): engine.signal or table-write resume for RuntimeContextWorkflowNative. BLOCKS all mailbox deletions.
- tf-vrz6 (Stage A, SDD deletion-bead #2) becomes BLOCKED on A; lands schema + workflow point-read + cursor + idempotent writes via A's primitive; may dual-write for wakeup only after B (and even then dispatch guardrail forbids ADDING a deferred bridge — so prefer landing after B).
- Then deletion beads #4-#8 (delete inputIntents / dispatcher / append-deferred / deferred-scan / reconcile), each blocked on A+B+tf-vrz6.
Chain: tf-9rpy <- tf-vrz6 <- {bead A, bead B}.

comments.author: gnijor
comments.text: STOPPED (ordering boundary) — draft PR #654 + docs/investigations/2026-05-22-tf-vrz6-p3-rows-ordering-boundary.md. EMPIRICAL: DurableTable.rows() is PRIMARY-KEY-sorted, not arrival-ordered (probe: inserted charlie/alpha/zulu/bravo -> rows() a/b/m/z). P3 keys inputs by inputId (non-monotonic), so P3-A 'tail inputs.rows() arrival-ordered projection' is false — a P3-A cursor would assign ordinals in inputId-lexical order = wrong delivery order (latent correctness bug). Forbidden escapes ruled out (write-time nextInputSequenceToAssign banned; acceptedAt unreliable; PK-order changes semantics). Only compliant path = P3-B: surface the durable-stream append offset (appendWithProducer Appended, discarded by insertOrGet at DurableTable.ts:787-789) on InsertOrGetResult — an ADDITIVE effect-durable-operators substrate change, beyond this runtime slice (dispatch/SDD A4 STOP). No smaller sub-slice (new-arrival discovery is the core). F3 wakeup half already proven (tf-e5rf/#651); this is the ordering half. Filed tf-qtfb (P3-B substrate); tf-vrz6 re-blocked on it. No production code changed.

comments.author: gnijor
comments.text:
STOP (resume attempt, 2026-05-22, off origin/main incl #658). Branch state was clean: removed stale STOP-era worktree/branch (0 commits, never pushed, off old main), re-entered fresh as codex/tf-vrz6-runtime-context-input-table-p3b. No production code changed.

EMPIRICAL FINDING THAT BLOCKS THE STATED SLICE: durable-stream offsets are NON-DENSE byte positions, not a dense per-message sequence. Probe (4 sequential + 4 concurrent insertOrGet on one stream, real @durable-streams/server, throwaway test removed) returned offsets whose logical part is 0141, 0282, 0423, 0564, 0705, 0846, 0987, 1128 — a constant +141 step (= serialized event byte size). So:
- The offset is a per-stream BYTE POSITION; the next offset = cursor + size(next event), unknowable in advance.
- "point-read arrival by offset" (get(cursor+1)) as the dispatch/STOP-doc framed it is IMPOSSIBLE. The offset is a valid TOTAL ORDER (monotonic, lexicographic via zero-pad — good, that is what #658 proved) but NOT a dense addressable key.
- One stream per TABLE LAYER (streamOptions.url), so the offset interleaves ALL collections (contexts, inputs, assignedInputs) of that table — like the tf-aseo events/logs shared-sequence. Input-arrival consumption must filter to input events.

CONSEQUENCE: arrival-ordered consumption is a FORWARD-ORDERED READ, not a point-get. Two candidate designs, both materially different from the stated slice and each with an open problem:
1) Offset-keyed arrival index: producer writes arrivals[ctx + "/" + paddedOffset] -> inputId from Inserted.offset; workflow walks arrivals.rows() (PK=paddedOffset => arrival-sorted) + durable cursor. OPEN: this is a producer-written index (the amendment specified a WORKFLOW-written assignedInputs ordinal, not a producer index), AND it has a CRASH HOLE — a producer crash between inputs.insertOrGet and the arrivals write loses the arrival entry permanently, because a retry gets Found which carries NO offset (#658: Found has no offset by design). Not crash-safe without more.
2) Raw-stream forward read: workflow reads the inputs collection underlying stream from its durable cursor offset forward (DurableOutputCursor-analog over the raw stream), filtering to input-insert events on the multiplexed per-table stream. Avoids the secondary-index crash hole, but is a different mechanism than the table API and needs the shared-sequence/gap-skip handling tf-aseo established.

WHY NOT IMPLEMENT NOW: the input arm is correctness-critical replay code at the heart of the engine/runtime-context SCC. The amendment + STOP-doc consume design ("point-read arrival by offset", "tail inputs.rows()") is now twice-invalidated (rows() PK-sorted per #654; offsets non-dense per this probe). Coding a production replay path on a just-invalidated, unvalidated design risks the exact latent ordering bug #654 warned about.

RECOMMENDATION:
A) Redo the tiny-firegrid derisk for the P3-B CONSUME shape (the existing tiny-input-append-wakeup used the SUPERSEDED write-time sequence/inputIds shape, per its FINDING.md — it does NOT validate P3-B arrival ordering). Prove: inputId-keyed inputs (idempotent, no write-time sequence) + arrival order from offset + workflow cursor + forward-ordered consume + crash-safety + replay reconstruction + suspend/resume, GREEN with a trace gate. This pins design #1 vs #2 and the crash-safety/compliance questions.
B) Amend SDD_RUNTIME_CONTEXT_WORKFLOW_INPUT_TABLE_CUTOVER Amendment 1 §A2: replace "point-read arrival by offset / tail inputs.rows()" with the validated forward-ordered-read mechanism (offsets are a non-dense total order; consume is a forward read after the cursor offset, not a point-get).
C) Only then do the production runtime cutover.

tf-vrz6 stays OPEN, blocked on the P3-B consume derisk (A). Chain: tf-9rpy <- tf-vrz6 <- (P3-B consume derisk + amendment fix). #658 (offset surfaced) and tf-e5rf/#651 (suspend/resume wakeup) remain valid building blocks.

title: Implement sparse workflow-owned output transition log for RuntimeContext body
description: Structural follow-on to tf-aseo/#664 and the 2026-05-22 reclassification of SDD_DURABLE_OUTPUT_CURSOR_PRIMITIVE. The dense raw-output DurableOutputCursor is a bridge/hotfix for tf-7kq8, not target architecture. RuntimeContextWorkflow transitionOutputEvent only acts on PermissionRequest, non-ACP ToolUse, and Terminated; Ready/TextChunk/Status/Error/TurnComplete are no-op cursor advancement. Implement the target shape: raw output stream remains for UI/session clients/telemetry, while the output appender/projector writes only body-relevant semantic transition events to a workflow-owned sparse output transition log and arms the owning RuntimeContext workflow using the kernel-owned write+arm class. Acceptance: runtime body no longer consumes the dense raw output stream for state transitions; terminal prompt completion can bind to the sparse transition/result log; no ACP edge-local Done synthesis over TurnComplete; no reintroduction of replay scans or output DurableDeferred mailbox. STOP if this crosses unresolved host-kernel/controller ownership or requires a generic engine suspended-workflow sweep.

title: Source-read: AgentOutputEvent _tag union schema replay durability — does runtimeAgentOutputObservationFromRow's Schema.decodeUnknown survive future schema variants on persisted rows?
description:
Per OLA's pipeline-layer de-risk proposal 2026-05-20.

QUESTION: runtimeAgentOutputObservationFromRow in runtime-output-journal.ts uses Schema.decodeUnknown. If AgentOutputEvent adds a variant in the future, do persisted rows still decode?

WORK (~20 min):
- Read packages/runtime/src/agent-event-pipeline/events/output.ts (the decode call site)
- Trace the AgentOutputEvent _tag union breadth
- Determine whether Schema.decodeUnknown is permissive on unknown variants (passes through) or strict (rejects)
- Verdict: PERMISSIVE-DECODE-SAFE or STRICT-DECODE-MIGRATION-RISK (with citation)

DELIVERABLE: short FINDING doc OR bead comment with the verdict + citations.

NON-CONFLICT: source-read only.
close_reason: DECIDED: shipped via PR #473. VERDICT STRICT-DECODE-MIGRATION-RISK — Schema.decodeUnknown is strict; future _tag union expansion would fail decode on persisted rows. Follow-up filed for schema migration path.

title: RuntimeContextEngineRegistry collapse: move from scoped engine per context to one host-scoped engine with many executions keyed by contextId/executionId
description:
Per Gurdas's P0 post-cutover dispatch + SDD update (main commit 6f7e398ee).

ARCHITECTURE TARGET: packages/host-sdk/src/host/runtime-context-engine-registry.ts

CRITICAL CLARIFICATION (different class from tf-ho99 control-request-reconciler):
- This file is ALREADY event-driven for input intents via table.inputIntents.rows() + Stream.runForEach. DO NOT regress to polling.
- The collapse target is the ENGINE OWNERSHIP MODEL, not the event-flow model.

CURRENT SHAPE:
- RuntimeContextEngineRegistry provisions/caches N scoped DurableStreamsWorkflowEngine instances for N contexts (one-engine-per-context)

TARGET SHAPE:
- ONE host-scoped workflow engine owns many runtime-context workflow executions, keyed by contextId/executionId

API TRANSFORMATIONS:
- claimActive → engine.ensureExecution(contextId)
- dispatchIntent → engine.signal(executionId, 'input', intent)
- deregister → engine.interrupt(executionId)
- Registry shrinks to contextId↔executionId mapping + SDK compatibility glue

SEQUENCING:
- NOT Phase 1, not a blocker for current fan-in
- Post-cutover / likely Phase 3 (unless engine-native primitives track activates sooner)
- DEPENDS ON:
  1. engine.signal(executionId, channel, payload) or equivalent primitive — currently NOT in the engine; would come from the engine-native track (tf-0mt5)
  2. Explicit one-engine-many-executions architectural decision
- KEEP RuntimeInputIntentDispatcherLive's event-driven subscription shape — possible recycle concern is dispatcher in-flight intent handling under host-scope close, NOT polling

RELATED:
- tf-ho99 (Reconciler-as-workflows) — sibling post-cutover collapse, different class (reconciler-loop vs engine-cardinality)
- tf-0mt5 (contingency anchor) — trigger #7 added for this target specifically
- packages/host-sdk/src/host/runtime-context-engine-registry.ts (target file)

SDD ANCHORS (in docs/sdds/SDD_FIREGRID_ENGINE_NATIVE_PRIMITIVES_ESCAPE_HATCH.md):
- §Post-Cutover Structural Collapse Targets — names this + tf-ho99 explicitly
- Decision trigger #7 — if post-cutover plan chooses this collapse, activate signal primitive + one-engine-many-executions design call

NOT YET DISPATCHED. Queued post-Phase-1-cutover. Coordinator monitors trigger #7 activation along with tf-0mt5's other 6 triggers.
close_reason: DECIDED: shipped via PR #494 (merge a89c3658b). RuntimeContextEngineRegistry app surface DELETED; replaced with Layer-provided services (RuntimeContextWorkflowRuntime / RuntimeContextInput / RuntimeContextCheckpointSource) over one host-scoped workflow engine. Preserved event-driven input-intent dispatch. SDD-named Post-Cutover Structural Collapse Target #2 of 2 (sibling to tf-ho99). Workflow engine now invisible infrastructure under Layer-provided capabilities — the CANONICAL EXAMPLE of the channels-as-Layers pattern that tf-kddg will mirror for Phase-3 channel restructure.

comments.author: codex
comments.text: ARCHITECTURE TIGHTENING 2026-05-20 (main commit 92ec6a295): Gurdas rejected the registry concept itself. RuntimeContextEngineRegistry is not just something to shrink; it should disappear as an architectural object or become a private Layer-construction adapter. Correct target: one host-scoped workflow engine provided through Effect Context/Layer, many executions keyed by contextId/executionId. Host/app code depends on Layer-provided runtime-context/channel capabilities, not on a registry object that hands out scoped engines. Any lookup table is allowed only at protocol/MCP string boundary, not as application model.

One host engine, many executions, but framed as invisible infra under public Layer-provided capabilities — not 'shrunk registry.' Apologies for the prior 'shrunk registry + SDK glue' framing.

title: TFIND-015: Permission flow and codec authority architectural decision
description:
Area: permissions / codecs.

Status: B IMPLEMENTED — #350 coordinator-APPROVED (2026-05-18); merge-gated on full GitHub CI green only.

Triage: cat-1/2 production architectural (resolved-direction: B). ACP codec currently does durable-deferred authority inside the codec (verified) vs README 'codecs may not own durable permission state' — B aligns impl to documented contract; TFIND-041 authority-family precedent.

Framing (SDD #350, by 163, coordinator-reviewed 2026-05-18): §0 = Gurdas-accepted question verbatim. A = bless ACP codec-side durable-deferred authority; B (recommended) = strict observation + workflow-side durable resumption.

Evidence (coordinator-verified): ACP codec genuinely does durable-deferred permission authority from inside the codec — DurableDeferred.make (acp/index.ts:154-158), engine.deferredResult poll + DurableDeferred.done (:283-328), requiring WorkflowEngine/WorkflowInstance — while codecs README explicitly states codecs 'may not own durable permission state.' Concrete documented-contract contradiction; B aligns impl to existing contract.

Verified in-diff: ACP codec drops WorkflowEngine/DurableDeferred (import line removed, no re-add); workflow owns the bridge via existing runtimeInputDeferredFor(contextId,seq) (no new wait primitive); deterministic test has real blocked-pending point BEFORE response ingress + exact runtime-context/<contextId>/input/1 deferred + no sleep-race; no forcing/widening cast.

On merge → toy permission-flow-pipeline capability EXISTS → next dispatchable toy build.
close_reason: TFIND-015 Build B implemented + MERGED in PR #350 (5ecc20d53): ACP codec stripped of durable permission authority, workflow owns the bridge via existing runtimeInputDeferredFor, deterministic emit-then-wait test (real blocked-pending point + exact deferred, no sleep-race), no forcing cast, SDD signed-off. Coordinator diff-verified. Gurdas D1 ruling 2026-05-18 = done; no separate pending kernel.

title: Design external MCP attachment path for provider tools before outbound channels
description:
Design the generic path for connecting external MCP tools to Firegrid-launched agents before we add provider-specific outbound action channels such as linear.issue.comment.create.

Context / architectural concern:
- PR #559 attempted to add a Linear issue comment callable channel in @firegrid/protocol/channels plus runtime/host Live bindings. Review disposition: wrong shape for core Firegrid; provider-specific outbound actions likely belong first as MCP tools, not Firegrid channels.
- Firegrid should provide durable agent runtime, session lifecycle, permissioning, observation, replay, waits, and traces around MCP tool use. It should not eagerly encode Linear/GitHub/Slack action semantics in the core protocol channel catalog.
- Promote a provider action into a Firegrid channel only when there is concrete pressure for Firegrid-owned durability semantics around the side effect itself: durable action rows, claim-before-side-effect, retries, idempotent receipt/evidence facts, or waitable completion facts.

References / architecture inspiration:
- Smithery Connect docs: https://smithery.ai/docs/use/connect#curl
  Key ideas to evaluate: hosted MCP connections, OAuth/config handling, namespace MCP endpoint, scoped service tokens, connection metadata, tool listing, prefixed tool names, and tool dispatch across connections.
- agent.pw reference: https://github.com/smithery-ai/agent.pw/blob/main/docs/reference.md
  Key ideas to evaluate: OAuth/header credential kinds, connect.prepare/startOAuth/completeOAuth/setHeaders/resolveHeaders, scoped credential-use rules, hosted OAuth handlers, and runtime header resolution.
- PR #559 review comments: do not bake Linear outbound action channels into core Firegrid; consider generic external MCP attachment instead.
- docs/sdds/SDD_FIREGRID_ONE_SUBSTRATE_PRIMITIVE.md external-effect adapter carveout and docs/sdds/SDD_FIREGRID_SESSION_FACT_CLIENT_SURFACES.md provider evidence paved road.

Scope:
1. Inventory current Firegrid MCP launch/config path: .mcp.json generation, runtime-context MCP server, agent adapter support for MCP config, and where Smithery namespace/server entries could be attached.
2. Propose a minimal Firegrid API/config shape for external MCP attachment, e.g. runtime config accepts MCP server descriptors or Smithery namespace/connection refs, which lower to the adapter-specific MCP config for Claude/Codex/ACP.
3. Define auth/credential boundary: what Firegrid stores, what Smithery/agent.pw stores, how scoped service tokens or resolved headers reach the agent runtime, and how to avoid leaking provider secrets into protocol/channel schemas.
4. Define observability/durability contract: how MCP tool calls and results appear in Firegrid output/events/traces; what Firegrid guarantees vs what remains provider/MCP-server responsibility.
5. Decide when outbound provider actions should remain MCP tools vs become Firegrid channels backed by durable action/evidence rows.
6. Produce a short design doc and backlog recommendations: close/retire tf-30nu as currently framed, replace with external-MCP attachment implementation bead(s), or keep a later provider-action-channel bead with explicit trigger conditions.

Acceptance:
- Design doc cites the Smithery and agent.pw references above.
- Recommends a concrete minimal API/config surface and package placement.
- Names at least one tiny-firegrid validation sim or smoke path.
- Updates or creates follow-up beads for implementation and any necessary SDD amendments.
- No code implementation in this bead unless limited to docs/backlog updates.
close_reason: DONE: PR #562 merged 02b73365. Design ratified: provider actions = MCP tools by default; promote to durable CallableChannel only on claim/receipt/retry/waitable-evidence (§4 crash test). RuntimeConfig.mcpServers IS the existing external-MCP attachment primitive. Real near-term gap: literal MCP header secrets in durable plane. Followups: followup-C (credential fix, P1), tf-30nu redirect (→ MCP-tool demo), followup-A (mcpServers docs), gated-B (McpConnectionResolver post-beta).

title: Fix MCP runtime-context tool publication for no-refresh clients
description: Existing findings already establish the root cause: Firegrid runtime-context MCP publishes the toolset progressively and emits notifications/tools/list_changed repeatedly; codex-acp snapshots tools once and has no list_changed refresh handler, so it sees only early tools while claude-acp refreshes and sees the full set. Required work: implement or narrowly design the Firegrid-side fix so the runtime-context MCP server exposes the complete canonical toolset synchronously before the first tools/list response, or otherwise make first tools/list stable for no-refresh MCP clients. Do not spend the task writing another findings report. Acceptance: source/test/trace evidence that first tools/list includes the full expected runtime-context toolset for startup, sleep, wait_for, send, wait_for_any, session_* and other canonical tools; list_changed storms are eliminated or made non-required for initial correctness; codex/no-refresh clients are not dependent on a later tools/list_changed refresh. STOP: if complete synchronous publication crosses runtime-context/MCP lifecycle architecture, report the exact boundary and minimal design decision needed.
close_reason: Completed: PR #670 merged; MCP toolkit registration now happens-before HTTP serving for no-refresh clients.

title: demo P0: thin ACP-shim forced first Firegrid tool_call for §6 planner (live §6 or hardened terminal finding)
close_reason: DECIDED: hardened terminal finding ACCEPTED — exemplary, not papered, decision-grade negative evidence. The thin ACP-shim (additive opt-in, substrate untouched) PROVABLY engaged (session_update ~6x) yet codex-acp still issued ZERO Firegrid tools/call (one codex-exploration title only), s6 0/6. Architectural boundary: an ACP-layer shim can refuse prose-only completion and re-drive the turn but CANNOT make the model CHOOSE a tool — tool_choice:required lives in the agent-internal model-request, unreachable from the ACP protocol layer. Combined with tf-549/#422 (native: no ACP runtime nor the ACP protocol exposes forced tool-invocation, source-verified) and tf-9q4 (cross-runtime), the conclusion is FULLY HARDENED & TERMINAL: Firegrid §6 substrate is provably complete; live agent-driven §6 is blocked SOLELY on agent-side forced tool-invocation that is, by construction, unreachable from the ACP protocol layer — a fundamental ACP-architecture/third-party limitation, NOT a Firegrid defect. Only path to a live full §6 = a non-ACP planner invocation with native tool_choice:required = a PO/Gurdas decision, not autonomous, not blocking the (complete, honest, research-grade) demo. oca3 shim angle CLOSED as negative evidence.

title: TFIND-031: Shared DurableTable tag-family provision missing (root cause)
description:
Area: host/toolkit composition.

Status: resolved (#331, e48d82904).

Triage: cat-2 (real host-composition correctness — missing shared DurableTable tag-family provision, was masked by the TFIND-005 any).

Surfaced by TFIND-005. Initially filed as 4 separate leaks (TFIND-031..034); Agent 2's diagnosis (2026-05-17) showed they are ONE root cause: all four production boundaries plus test fallout leak the same tag family (RuntimeControlPlaneTable, RuntimeOutputTable, and four DurableWait* row tags). The any collapsed every consumer's requirements channel, so a single missing provision site in the host/toolkit composition manifested at 4 production type-boundaries + test boundaries.

Resolution: Option-Y execution-scoped self-containment — support layer required what it provided. Fix (NO cast): provide the SAME HostRuntimeObservationSubstrateLive reference INTO the executor while keeping it provideMerged into the workflow chain → Effect memoization = ONE shared store (workflow body + wait-router + tool executor); toolCallWorkflowSupportLayer got analogous self-containment.

A first sibling-Layer.merge attempt typechecked but broke TOOL_EXECUTOR_SEAM.2 schedule_me — caught and rejected by deterministic record→blocked→wake test, not forced green; corrected re-thread passes it. Full gate green; closes the TFIND-005 leak-stack root.
